lineage: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
Of the Literary Labors of the Two Apollinares and the Emperor's Prohibition of Christians being instructed in Greek Literature.

ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: Of the Literary Labors of the Two Apollinares and the Emperor's Prohibition of Christians being instructed in Greek Literature.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The imperial law which forbade Christians to study Greek literature, rendered the two Apollinares of whom we have above spoken, much more distinguished than before.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For both being skilled in polite learning, the father as a grammarian, and the son as a rhetorician, they made themselves serviceable to the Christians at this crisis.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For the former, as a grammarian, composed a grammar consistent with the Christian faith: he also translated the Books of Moses into heroic verse; and paraphrased all the historical books of the Old Testament, putting them partly into dactylic measure, and partly reducing them to the form of dramatic tragedy.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He purposely employed all kinds of verse, that no form of expression peculiar to the Greek language might be unknown or unheard of amongst Christians.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The younger Apollinaris, who was well trained in eloquence, expounded the gospels and apostolic doctrines in the way of dialogue, as Plato among the Greeks had done.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Thus showing themselves useful to the Christian cause they overcame the subtlety of the emperor through their own labors.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But Divine Providence was more potent than either their labors, or the craft of the emperor: for not long afterwards, in the manner we shall hereafter explain, the law became wholly inoperative; and the works of these men are now of no greater importance, than if they had never been written.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But perhaps some one will vigorously reply saying: ‘On what grounds do you affirm that both these things were effected by the providence of God?

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: That the emperor’s sudden death was very advantageous to Christianity is indeed evident: but surely the rejection of the Christian compositions of the two Apollinares, and the Christians beginning afresh to imbue their minds with the philosophy of the heathens, this works out no benefit to Christianity, for pagan philosophy teaches Polytheism, and is injurious to the promotion of true religion.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This objection I shall meet with such considerations as at present occur to me.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Greek literature certainly was never recognized either by Christ or his Apostles as divinely inspired, nor on the other hand was it wholly rejected as pernicious.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: And this they did, I conceive, not inconsiderately.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For there were many philosophers among the Greeks who were not far from the knowledge of God; and in fact these being disciplined by logical science, strenuously opposed the Epicureans and other contentious Sophists who denied Divine Providence, confuting their ignorance.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: And for these reasons they have become useful to all lovers of real piety: nevertheless they themselves were not acquainted with the Head of true religion, being ignorant of the mystery of Christ which ‘had been hidden from generations and ages.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: And that this was so, the Apostle in his epistle to the Romans thus declares: ‘For the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of men, who hold the truth in unrighteousness.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Because that which may be known of God is manifest in them; for God has shown it unto them.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For the invisible things of him from the creation of the world are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made, even his eternal power and Godhead, that they may be without excuse; because that when they knew God, they glorified him not as God.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: From these words it appears that they had the knowledge of truth, which God had manifested to them; but were guilty on this account, that when they knew God, they glorified him not as God.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Wherefore by not forbidding the study of the learned works of the Greeks, they left it to the discretion of those who wished to do so.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This is our first argument in defense of the position we took: another may be thus put: The divinely inspired Scriptures undoubtedly inculcate doctrines that are both admirable in themselves, and heavenly in their character: they also eminently tend to produce piety and integrity of life in those who are guided by their precepts, pointing out a walk of faith which is highly approved of God.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But they do not instruct us in the art of reasoning, by means of which we may be enabled successfully to resist those who oppose the truth.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Besides adversaries are most easily foiled, when we can use their own weapons against them.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But this power was not supplied to Christians by the writings of the Apollinares.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Julian had this in mind when he by law prohibited Christians from being educated in Greek literature, for he knew very well that the fables it contains would expose the whole pagan system, of which he had become the champion to ridicule and contempt.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Even Socrates, the most celebrated of their philosophers, despised these absurdities, and was condemned on account of it, as if he had attempted to violate the sanctity of their deities.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Moreover, both Christ and his Apostle enjoin us ‘to become discriminating money-changers,’ so that we might ‘prove all things, and hold fast that which is good’: directing us also to ‘beware lest any one should spoil us through philosophy and vain deceit.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But this we cannot do, unless we possess ourselves of the weapons of our adversaries: taking care that in making this acquisition we do not adopt their sentiments, but testing them, reject the evil, but retain all that is good and true: for good wherever it is found, is a property of truth.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Should any one imagine that in making these assertions we wrest the Scriptures from their legitimate construction, let it be remembered that the Apostle not only does not forbid our being instructed in Greek learning, but that he himself seems by no means to have neglected it, inasmuch as he knows many of the sayings of the Greeks.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Whence did he get the saying, ‘The Cretans are always liars, evil beasts, slow-bellies,’ but from a perusal of The Oracles of Epimenides, the Cretan Initiator?

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Or how would he have known this, ‘For we are also his offspring,’ had he not been acquainted with The Phenomena of Aratus the astronomer?

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Again this sentence, ‘Evil communications corrupt good manners,’ is a sufficient proof that he was conversant with the tragedies of Euripides.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But what need is there of enlarging on this point?

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: It is well known that in ancient times the doctors of the church by unhindered usage were accustomed to exercise themselves in the learning of the Greeks, until they had reached an advanced age: this they did with a view to improve themselves in eloquence and to strengthen and polish their mind, and at the same time to enable them to refute the errors of the heathen.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Let these remarks be sufficient in the subject suggested by the two Apollinares.
